questions.question_text: Study of rate of chemical reaction is known as
questions.question_html: Study of rate of chemical reaction is known as
questions.explanation: Chemical kinetics is the branch of chemistry which deals with the study of rate of reaction, factors affecting the rate of reaction and mechanism of the chemical reaction.

questions.question_text: Which among the following is an instantaneous reaction?
questions.question_html: Which among the following is an instantaneous reaction?

options.text: Burning of a log of wood
options.html: Burning of a log of wood

questions.explanation: Mixing of silver nitrate and sodium chloride involves ionic reaction to form precipitates of silver chloride. {tex}AgNO_3(aq)\;+NaCl(aq)\:\rightarrow AgCl(s)\;+\;NaNO_3(aq){/tex} Since ionic reactions are very fast, so it is an instantaneous reaction.

questions.question_text: Rate of reaction for the combustion of propane is equal to: {tex}{C_3}{H_8}(g) + 5{O_2}(g) \to 3C{O_2}(g) + 4{H_2}O(g){/tex}

Rate of reaction for the combustion of propane is equal to:
{tex}{C_3}{H_8}(g) + 5{O_2}(g) \to 3C{O_2}(g) + 4{H_2}O(g){/tex}

options.html: {tex}- \frac{{\vartriangle [{C_3}{H_8}]}}{{\vartriangle t}} = - \frac{1}{5}\frac{{\vartriangle [{O_2}]}}{{\vartriangle t}} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{\vartriangle [C{O_2}]}}{{\vartriangle t}}{/tex} {tex} = + \frac{1}{4}\frac{{\vartriangle [{H_2}O]}}{{\vartriangle t}}{/tex}

questions.explanation: Rate of reaction= (-) Rate of disappearance of reactants = (+) Rate of the appearance of products. Where negative sign indicates that the concentration of reactants decreases and a positive sign indicates that the concentration of products increases.

questions.question_text: What is the order of a reaction which has a rate expression ; Rate = {tex}k\left[A\right]^\frac32\left[B\right]^1{/tex}
questions.question_html: What is the order of a reaction which has a rate expression ; Rate = {tex}k\left[A\right]^\frac32\left[B\right]^1{/tex}
questions.explanation: Order of a reaction may be defined as sum of the powers of the concentration of each reactant in the rate law expression. therefore, order of this reaction is {tex}\frac32+1=\;\frac52=\;2\frac12{/tex}

questions.question_text: Molecularity of a reaction
questions.question_html: Molecularity of a reaction
questions.explanation: Molecularity means minimum no. of atoms molecules or ions which undergo collision for the conversion of reactant to Product. So it is a whole no. Cannot be fractional or negative.

questions.question_html: For which of the following reaction the units of rate constant and rate of the reaction are same?
questions.explanation: {tex}Rate = k [Reactant]^0{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of the following statement is true for order of a reaction?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statement is true for order of a reaction?

options.text: It can be written from the balanced chemical equation.
options.html: It can be written from the balanced chemical equation.

options.text: It depends on the stoichiometric coefficients of reactants as well as products.
options.html: It depends on the stoichiometric coefficients of reactants as well as products.

questions.explanation: The order of a reaction is experimentally determined quantity. So it can be whole number value, negative, fractional, or zero.

questions.question_text: For a reaction; pA +qB products, the rate law expression is {tex}{\text{r}}\,\,{\text{ = }}\,\,{\text{k}}{\left[ {\text{A}} \right]^{\text{l}}}{\left[ {\text{B}} \right]^{\text{m}}}{/tex} . Which among the following statement is true for this expression?
questions.question_html: For a reaction; pA +qB products, the rate law expression is  {tex}{\text{r}}\,\,{\text{ = }}\,\,{\text{k}}{\left[ {\text{A}} \right]^{\text{l}}}{\left[ {\text{B}} \right]^{\text{m}}}{/tex}. Which among the following statement is true for this expression?

options.text: (p+q) may or may not be equal to (l+m)
options.html: (p+q) may or may not be equal to (l+m)

questions.correct_option_text: (p+q) may or may not be equal to (l+m)
questions.explanation: The order of a reaction is experimentally determined quantity. It can be calculated as the sum of the power of each reactant in rate law expression. So, It may or may not be equal to stoichiometric coefficients.

questions.question_text: For the first order reaction, half life is equal to
questions.question_html: For the first order reaction, half life is equal to
questions.explanation: Half-life period means the time when the concentration of reactant becomes half to the original concentration of the reactant. for first-order reaction rate constant can be calculated as follows: {tex}{t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{\left[ {{A_0}} \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]}}}{/tex} for the half-life period, {tex}{t = {t_{1{/_2}}},\left[ A \right] = \frac{{\left[ {{A_0}} \right]}}{2}}{/tex} on solving, {tex}{{t_{1{/_2}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{\left[ {{A_0}} \right]}}{{\frac{{\left[ {{A_0}} \right]}}{2}}}}{/tex} {tex}{{t_{1{/_2}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 2 = \frac{{2.303 \times 0.3010}}{k}}{/tex} {tex}{{t_{1{/_2}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}}{/tex} where letters have their usual meanings.

questions.question_text: Straight line graph for first order reaction is obtained between
questions.question_html: Straight line graph for first order reaction is obtained between
questions.explanation: We know that, {tex}\begin{array}{l}t=\frac1k\ln\frac{\left[R_0\right]}{\left[R\right]}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}Ln\left[ R \right] = ln\left[ {{R_o}} \right] - kt{/tex} on comparing with equation of line, y=mx+c y=ln[R] , x=t, m=slope= -k, intercept= ln[R 0 ] graph between ln[R] and t will be straight line with negative slope.

questions.question_text: The effect of temperature on reaction rate is given by
questions.question_html: The effect of temperature on reaction rate is given by
questions.explanation: Arrhenius gives a relation between rate constant (k) and temperature (T) is as follows: {tex}{k = A\;{e^{\frac{{ - {E_a}}}{T}}}}{/tex} taking log on both sides, we get. {tex}{\ln k = \ln A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{T}}{/tex} {tex}Ln\left[ R \right] = ln\left[ {{R_o}} \right] - kt{/tex}

questions.question_text: The chemical reaction in which reactants require high amount of activation energy are generally
questions.question_html: The chemical reaction in which reactants require high amount of activation energy are generally
questions.explanation: As the energy of activation increases, more energy is required for reactants to complete the reaction hence chemical reaction become slow.

options.html: Increase in number of collisions between molecules having energy greater than activation energy

questions.correct_option_text: Increase in number of collisions between molecules having energy greater than activation energy
questions.explanation: With increase in temperature molecular collisions increases therefore rate of reaction doubles. With every 10 ° rises in temperature, the number of molecules which bring about the chemical reaction becomes double, so rate of chemical reaction also double.

questions.explanation: It describes the speed of a reaction i.e. how the concentration of a reactant changes w.r.t. time. Hence it is called Velocity constant.

questions.question_text: The radioactive isotope used in determining the age of organic substances is
questions.question_html: The radioactive isotope used in determining the age of organic substances is
questions.explanation: The ratio of Carbon -12 to Carbon-14 at the moment of death is the same as every other living thing., but Carbon-14 decays and is not replaced. The carbon-14 decays with its half-life of 5770 years., while the amount of Carbon-12 remains constant in the sample. By looking at the ratio of Carbon-12 to Carbon-14 in the sample and comparing it to the ratio in a living organism, it is possible to determine the age of a formerly living thing fairly precisely.

questions.explanation: Catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant it only helps in reaching the equilibrium at a faster rate,i.e. it catalyzes the forward as well as the backward reactions to the same extent so that the equilibrium state remains the same but is reached earlier.

questions.explanation: Pre-exponential factor 'A' is part of the Arrhenius equation, which is formulated by Swedish chemist Savante Arrhenius in 1889. It is also known as Arrhenius factor and frequency factor which represents the frequency of collisions between reacting molecules.

questions.explanation: Catalyst provides the low activation energy path for completion of reaction.

In the catalyzed decomposition of benzene diazonium chloride,

questions.question_text: Activation energy of a reaction is
questions.question_html: Activation energy of a reaction is

options.text: The energy released during the reaction
options.html: The energy released during the reaction

options.text: The energy absorbed during a reaction
options.html: The energy absorbed during a reaction

options.text: Energy evolved when activated complex is formed

options.text: The minimum amount of energy required to overcome the barrier
options.html: The minimum amount of energy required to overcome the barrier

questions.correct_option_text: The minimum amount of energy required to overcome the barrier
questions.explanation: The minimum extra amount of energy required by the reactants to form the activated complex is known as activation energy. In terms of the transition-state theory, the activation energy is the difference in energy content between atoms or molecules in an activated or transition-state configuration and the corresponding atoms.

questions.question_text: In a reaction, 2A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} products, the concentration of A decreases from 0.5 mol/L to 0.4mol/L in 10 mins. Calculate the rate during this interval.
questions.question_html: In a reaction, 2A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} products, the concentration of A decreases from 0.5 mol/L to 0.4mol/L in 10 mins. Calculate the rate during this interval.
questions.explanation: for 2A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} products rate = {tex}{- \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d[A]}}{{dt}} = - \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{0.4-0.5}}{{10}}}{/tex} rate interval = 0.005 mol L -1 min -1

questions.question_text: For a chemical reaction 2X + Y {tex}\to{/tex} Z, the rate of appearance of Z is 0.05 mol L –1 min –1 . The rate of disappearance of X will be:
questions.question_html: For a chemical reaction 2X + Y {tex}\to{/tex} Z, the rate of appearance of Z is 0.05 mol L–1min–1. The rate of disappearance of X will be:
questions.explanation: 2X + Y {tex}\to{/tex} Z hence, rate expression for the reaction will be rate = {tex}{ - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d[X]}}{{dt}} = - \frac{{d[Y]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d[Z]}}{{dt}}}{/tex} {tex}{\frac{{d[X]}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{d[Z]}}{{dt}}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 = 0.1mol L -1 min -1

questions.question_text: In the formation of sulphur trioxide 2SO 2 (g) + O 2 (g) {tex} \rightleftarrows{/tex} 2SO 3 (g). The rate of reaction is expressed as {tex}{\text{ - }}\frac{{{\text{d[O2]}}}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = 2.5\times{10^{ - 4}}{\text{mol }}{{\text{L}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{{\text{s}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex} . The rate of disappearance of SO 2 will be
questions.question_html: In the formation of sulphur trioxide 2SO2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftarrows{/tex} 2SO3(g). The rate of reaction is expressed as {tex}{\text{ - }}\frac{{{\text{d[O2]}}}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = 2.5\times{10^{ - 4}}{\text{mol }}{{\text{L}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{{\text{s}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}{/tex} . The rate of disappearance of SO2 will be
questions.explanation: 2SO 2 ​(g) + O 2 (g) ⇌ 2SO 2 ​(g) rate = {tex}{ - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d[S{O_2}]}}{{dt}} = - \frac{{d[{O_2}]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d[S{O_3}]}}{{dt}}}{/tex} {tex}{\frac{{d[S{O_2}]}}{{dt}} = 2 \times \frac{{d[{O_2}]}}{{dt}} }{/tex} {tex}= 2 \times 2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} ={/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1

questions.question_text: Following mechanism has been proposed for a reaction 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} D + E A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D (slow) A + C {tex}\to{/tex} E (fast) The rate law expression for the reaction is
questions.question_html:
Following mechanism has been proposed for a reaction
2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} D + E
A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D (slow)
A + C {tex}\to{/tex} E (fast)
The rate law expression for the reaction is
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction is determined by slowest step.

questions.explanation: For a first order reaction, {tex}t=\frac{2.303}k\log\frac a{a-x}=\frac{2.303}k\log\frac a{\displaystyle\frac a4}=\frac{2.303}k\log4{/tex}

questions.question_text: If 75% of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min, then 50% of the reaction was completed in_____.
questions.question_html: If 75% of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min, then 50% of the reaction was completed in_____.
questions.explanation: 75% completion means 2 half lifes so 50% completion means only one half life. {tex}{k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}}{/tex} {tex}{k = \frac{{2.303}}{{32}}\log \left( {\frac{{100}}{{100 - 75}}} \right)...(1)}{/tex} {tex}{k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \left( {\frac{{100}}{{100 - 50}}} \right)...(2)}{/tex} from (1) and (2), we get t = 16 mins

questions.question_text: Milk turns sour at 40 ° C three times faster than it does at 0 ° C. this shows that activation energy of souring of milk (in cal) is
questions.question_html: Milk turns sour at 40°C three times faster than it does at 0°C. this shows that activation energy of souring of milk (in cal) is

questions.question_text: The rate constant of a reaction is 0.0693 min -1 . Starting with 10 mol L -1 , the rate of reaction after 10 min will be:
questions.question_html: The rate constant of a reaction is 0.0693 min-1. Starting with 10 mol L-1, the rate of reaction after 10 min will be:
questions.explanation: Use relation for first-order kinetics {tex}{t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}}{/tex} {tex}{{t_{{1_{{/_2}}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{k} = \frac{{0.693}}{{0.0693}} = 10}{/tex} Since the half-life period of reaction= 10 min and initial concentration=10mol. Then concentration after 10min= 5 mol (50% ). rate of reaction = k {tex}\times{/tex} concentration at time t = 0.0693 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 0.3465 mol L -1 min -1 .

questions.question_text: In the presence of a catalyst, the activation energy of a reaction is lowered by 2 kcal at 27 ° C. The rate of reaction will increase by
questions.question_html: In the presence of a catalyst, the activation energy of a reaction is lowered by 2 kcal at 27°C. The rate of reaction will increase by
questions.explanation: {tex}{K_1} = A\exp \left( {\frac{{ - E{a_1}}}{{RT}}} \right){/tex} and {tex}{K_2} = A\exp \left( {\frac{{ - E{a_2}}}{{RT}}} \right){/tex} in the absence of catalyst {tex}\log k=\log A-\frac{E_a}{2.303\;RT}.....(1){/tex} in the presence of catalyst, {tex}\log k_1=\;\log A\;-\left(\frac{E_a-2}{2.303RT}\right).....(2){/tex} subtracting (1) from(2), we get {tex}{\log \frac{{{k_1}}}{k} = \left( {\frac{{2kcal}}{{2.303RT}}} \right)}{/tex} {tex}{\log \frac{{{k_1}}}{k} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 4.2 \times 1000}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300}}} \right)}{/tex} {tex}{\log \frac{{{k_1}}}{k} = 1.46}{/tex} taking antilog on both sides, K 1 = 28k

questions.question_text: The half-life of a reaction is halved as the initial concentration of the reactant is doubled. The order of the reaction is:
questions.question_html: The half-life of a reaction is halved as the initial concentration of the reactant is doubled. The order of the reaction is:
questions.explanation: For 2 nd order reaction, the half-life is inversely related to the concentration of the reactant. t 1/2 for second order reaction {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{{\left[ R \right]}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The rate constant of the reaction at temperature 200 K is 10 times less than the rate constant at 400 K. The activation energy of the reaction is:
questions.question_html: The rate constant of the reaction at temperature 200 K is 10 times less than the rate constant at 400 K. The activation energy of the reaction is:

questions.question_text: The slope in the log k vs. {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} curve is 5.42 × 10 3 . The value of the activation energy is approximately
questions.question_html: The slope in the log k vs. {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex}  curve is 5.42 × 103. The value of the activation energy is approximately
questions.explanation: {tex}lnK = lnA - \frac{{Ea}}{{RT}}{/tex} on comparing with y=mx+c {tex}{slope = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}}{/tex} {tex}{{E_a} = 5.42 \times {{10}^3} \times 2.303 \times 8.314}{/tex} {tex}{{E_a} = 103.7KJ/mol}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The half-life periods of a reaction at initial concentration of 0.1 mol/L and 0.5 mol/L are 200 s and 40 s respectively. The order of the reaction is
questions.question_html: The half-life periods of a reaction at initial concentration of 0.1 mol/L and 0.5 mol/L are 200 s and 40 s respectively. The order of the reaction is
questions.explanation: As initial concentration has increased half-life is decreasing so the order of the reaction is 2. for second-order reaction, {tex}rate\;\propto\;\frac1{\left[R\right]}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The half life of a substance in a first order reaction is 15 min. The rate constant is
questions.question_html: The half life of a substance in a first order reaction is 15 min. The rate constant is
questions.explanation: for first order reaction {tex}t_{1_{/_2}}=\;\frac{0.693}k{/tex}

questions.question_text: The reaction {tex}2A \to B{/tex} is first order in A with a rate constant of 2.8 determining 10 –2 s –1 . How long will it take for A to decrease from 0.88 M to 0.14 M?
questions.question_html: The reaction {tex}2A \to B{/tex} is first order in A with a rate constant of 2.8 determining 10–2s–1. How long will it take for A to decrease from 0.88 M to 0.14 M?

questions.question_text: When 10 g of radioactive isotope is reduced to 1.25 g in 12 years, the half life period of the isotope is
questions.question_html: When 10 g of radioactive isotope is reduced to 1.25 g in 12 years, the half life period of the isotope is
questions.explanation: Radioactivity follow 1st order kinetics. {tex}{k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}}{/tex} {tex}{k = \frac{{2.303}}{{12}}\log \frac{{10}}{{1.25}}}{/tex} k = 0.1919 log 8 k = 0.1919 {tex}\times{/tex} log 2 3 k = 0.1919 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} log2 = 0.575 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3010 = 0.173yr -1 now, {tex}{{t_{{1_{{/_2}}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{0.173}} = 4.005yrs \simeq 4yrs}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of decay constant of a compound having a half life period of 2.95 days is
questions.question_html: The value of decay constant of a compound having a half life period of 2.95 days is
questions.explanation: Decay constant(k) = {tex}\frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex} {tex}k=\;\frac{0.693}{2.95\times24\times60\times60}=2.71\times10^{-6}\;s^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The reaction {tex}2{\text{ }}NO{\text{ }} + {\text{ }}B{r_2} \to 2NOBr{/tex} follows the mechanism given below: {tex}NO + B{r_2} \rightleftarrows NOB{r_2}(fast){/tex} NOBr 2 + NO {tex}\to{/tex} 2NOBr (slow) If the concentration of both NO and Br 2 is increased two times, the rate of reaction would become:
questions.question_html:
The reaction {tex}2{\text{ }}NO{\text{ }} + {\text{ }}B{r_2} \to 2NOBr{/tex} follows the mechanism given below:
{tex}NO + B{r_2} \rightleftarrows NOB{r_2}(fast){/tex}
NOBr2 + NO {tex}\to{/tex} 2NOBr (slow)
If the concentration of both NO and Br2 is increased two times, the rate of reaction would become:
questions.explanation: {tex}NO + B{r_2} \leftrightarrow NOB{r_2}[Fast,reversible]{/tex} NOBr 2 + NO {tex}\to{/tex} 2NOBr [Slow, RDS] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Rate = Rate 2 = k 2 [NO][NOBr 2 ] {tex}{ \to Rate1 = Rat{e_{ - 1}} \to {k_1}[NO][B{r_2}]}{/tex} {tex}{ = {k_{ - 1}}[NOB{r_2}]}{/tex} {tex}{ \to [NOB{r_2}] = ({k_1}/{k_{ - 1}})[NO][B{r_2}]}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow Rate = {k_2}[NO][NOB{r_2}]}{/tex} {tex}{ = {k_2}[NO]({k_1}/{k_{ - 1}})[NO][B{r_2}]}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow Rate = ({k_2}{k_1}/{k_{ - 1}}){{[NO]}^{2[}}B{r_2}]}{/tex} {tex}{ = k{{[NO]}^2}[B{r_2}]}{/tex} Rate=k[NO] 2 [Br 2 ], since rate of reaction w.r.t [NO] is second order and w.r.t [Br] is first order, then rate of reaction become 8times when conc. of [NO] and [Br] is doubled. rate ' = k[2NO] 2 [2Br 2 ] rate ' = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} Rate

questions.question_text: E a for the reaction is 1.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J/mol. The slope of the graph of log k vs. 1/T is
questions.question_html: Ea for the reaction is 1.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 105J/mol. The slope of the graph of log k vs. 1/T is
questions.explanation: {tex}lnK = lnA - \frac{{Ea}}{{RT}}{/tex} on comparing with y = mx + c graph between logK and 1/T is a straight line with negative slope. {tex}{slope = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}}{/tex} {tex}{slope = - \frac{{1.18 \times {{10}^5}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}}{/tex} slope = -6162.8

questions.question_text: For the reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} products, at {tex}\left[ A \right]{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0.4{\text{ }}M,{\text{ }}{t_{1/2}} = 24{\text{ }}s{/tex} and at {tex}\left[ A \right]{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0.2{\text{ }}M,{\text{ }}{t_{1/2}} = {\text{ }}12{\text{ }}s{/tex} . The unit for the rate constant is
questions.question_html: For the reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} products, at {tex}\left[ A \right]{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0.4{\text{ }}M,{\text{ }}{t_{1/2}} = 24{\text{ }}s{/tex} and at {tex}\left[ A \right]{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0.2{\text{ }}M,{\text{ }}{t_{1/2}} = {\text{ }}12{\text{ }}s{/tex}. The unit for the rate constant is
questions.explanation: The order of reaction will be zero because as concentration is decreased half-life is also decreasing so the unit of rate constant is {tex}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: For the reaction {tex}A\leftrightarrow B,\;\triangle H=+40kJ/mol{/tex} . IF E a for the forward reaction is 60 kJ/mol. E a for the backward reaction is
questions.question_html: For the reaction {tex}A\leftrightarrow B,\;\triangle H=+40kJ/mol{/tex}. IF Ea for the forward reaction is 60 kJ/mol. Ea for the backward reaction is
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta H = {E_{af}} - {E_{ab}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 40 = 60 - {E_{ab}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \,{E_{ab}} = 60 - 40 = 20\,KJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}{\vartriangle H = \;{E_{{a_f}}} - {E_{{a_b}}}}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow 40 = 60\; - {E_{{a_b}}}}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow {E_{{a_b}}} = 60 - 40 = 20\;KJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: For the reaction A + 3B {tex} \to {/tex} 2C + 2D, the concentration of A changes from 0.150 M to 0.0135 M in 1 min. The rate of formation of C in mol/L/s is:
questions.question_html: For the reaction A + 3B {tex} \to {/tex} 2C + 2D, the concentration of A changes from 0.150 M to 0.0135 M in 1 min. The rate of formation of C in mol/L/s is:
questions.explanation: {tex}{rate = - \frac{{d\left[ A \right]}}{{dt}} = - \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ B \right]}}{{dt}} = }{/tex} {tex}{ + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ C \right]}}{{dt}} = + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ D \right]}}{{dt}}}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow \frac{{d\left[ A \right]}}{{dt}} = - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ C \right]}}{{dt}}}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow \frac{{d\left[ C \right]}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{d\left[ A \right]}}{{dt}} = }{/tex} {tex}{ - \frac{{2 \times \left( {0.0135 - 0.0150} \right)}}{{1 \times 60}} = 5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}\;mol\;{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}}{/tex} rate = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 mol L -1 s -1

questions.question_text: Thermal decomposition of a compound is of first order. If 50% of a sample of a compound is decomposed in 120 min, the time taken for 90% completion is
questions.question_html: Thermal decomposition of a compound is of first order. If 50% of a sample of a compound is decomposed in 120 min, the time taken for 90% completion is
questions.explanation: Half-life of reaction = 120 min {tex}{t_{99.9}} = 10{\text{ }} \times {\text{ }}{t_{1/2}}{/tex} detail: here, {tex}{k = \frac{{0.693}}{{120}}}{/tex} also, {tex}{t = \frac{{2.303 \times 120}}{{0.693}}\log }{/tex} {tex}{{{10}^3} = \frac{{2.303 \times 120 \times 3}}{{0.693}}\log 10}{/tex} {tex}{ \Rightarrow t = \frac{{2.303 \times 120 \times 3 \times 1}}{{0.693}} = 399 }{/tex}

questions.question_text: In the catalyzed decomposition of benzene diazonium chloride, Half life period is found to be independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. After 10 min, the volume of N 2 gas collected is 10 L and after the reaction is complete, it is 50 L. Hence, the rate constant of the reaction(in min –1 ) is
questions.question_html:
In the catalyzed decomposition of benzene diazonium chloride,

Half life period is found to be independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. After 10 min, the volume of N2 gas collected is 10 L and after the
reaction is complete, it is 50 L. Hence, the rate constant of the reaction(in min–1) is
questions.explanation: it follows the 1st order reaction,hence {tex}k=\;\frac{2.303}t\log\frac{a_\circ}{a_\circ-x\;}=\frac{2.303}{10}\log\frac{50}{50-10}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Decomposition of H 2 O 2 was studied by titration against KMnO 4 solution. It was found that 0.4 mol of H 2 O 2 was reduced to 0.2 mol in 20 min and to 0.1 mol in 40 min and to 0.05 mol after 1 hr. the order of reaction must be
questions.question_html: Decomposition of H2O2 was studied by titration against KMnO4 solution. It was found that 0.4 mol of H2O2 was reduced to 0.2 mol in 20 min and to 0.1 mol in 40 min and to 0.05 mol after 1 hr. the order of reaction must be
questions.explanation: as concentration is decreasing regularly with time, so Decomposition of H 2 O 2 is 1st order.
